channel.title: Tiny Tech
channel.description: Step into the world of nanotechnology with Tiny Tech. Tiny Tech is a series of radio modules focusing on the science and engineering of the very small.
channel.itunes:author: lmwhite.chem.ufl.edu
channel.itunes:subtitle: Small Things. Big Changes.
channel.itunes:summary: Step into the world of nanotechnology with Tiny Tech. Tiny Tech is a series of radio modules focusing on the science and engineering of the very small.

itunes:owner.itunes:name: University of Florida Center for Nanostructured Electronic Materials

item.title: What is Nanotechnology?
item.itunes:author: Tiny Tech Radio Series
item.itunes:subtitle: Let's talk small, really small.
item.itunes:summary:
Episode 001
Let's talk small, really small. The field of nanotechnology is making big advances toward achieving precise control of matter on the nanometer scale. A nanometer is one billionth of a meter, or about the size of four adjacent atoms.

item.title: The Impact of Nanotechnology
item.itunes:author: Tiny Tech Radio Series
item.itunes:subtitle: We live in a world of change, and the technology that surrounds us is changing especially fast. One important contributor is something called nanotechnology.
item.itunes:summary:
Episode 002
We live in a world of change, and the technology that surrounds us is changing especially fast. One important contributor is something called nanotechnology.

item.title: The Single-Electron Transistor
item.itunes:author: Tiny Tech Radio Series
item.itunes:subtitle: Transistors are everywhere: in computers, in cell phones, and even in those gimmicky greeting cards that play tunes when they're opened.
item.itunes:summary:
Episode 003
Transistors are everywhere: in computers, in cell phones, and even in those gimmicky greeting cards that play tunes when they're opened.

item.title: Nanoelectromechanical Systems
item.itunes:author: Tiny Tech Radio Series
item.itunes:subtitle: In the 1966 movie Fantastic Voyage, Hollywood star Raquel Welch was part of a surgical team that was placed aboard a submarine, miniaturized to microscopic size, and injected into the bloodstream of a man.
item.itunes:summary:
Episode 004
In the 1966 movie Fantastic Voyage, Hollywood star Raquel Welch was part of a surgical team that was placed aboard a submarine, miniaturized to microscopic size, and injected into the bloodstream of a man.

item.title: Gold Nanoparticles: Not Just Tiny!
item.itunes:author: Tiny Tech Radio Series
item.itunes:subtitle: What would happen if you were to take a gold coin and cut it into two pieces, and then into four, and kept going until you had very tiny pieces consisting of perhaps a thousand gold atoms each?
item.itunes:summary:
Episode 005
What would happen if you were to take a gold coin and cut it into two pieces, and then into four, and kept going until you had very tiny pieces consisting of perhaps a thousand gold atoms each?

item.title: More on Moore's Law
item.itunes:author: Tiny Tech Radio Series
item.itunes:subtitle: In 1965, the chemist Gordon Moore predicted that the number of transistors on a computer chip would double every two years, due to improvements in technology to make the individual circuit components smaller and smaller.
item.itunes:summary:
Episode 006
In 1965, the chemist Gordon Moore predicted that the number of transistors on a computer chip would double every two years, due to improvements in technology to make the individual circuit components smaller and smaller.

item.title: Viruses as Nanotechnology
item.itunes:author: Tiny Tech Radio Series
item.itunes:subtitle: Feeling sick? Although many illnesses are caused by microorganisms, other diseases such as cystic fibrosis and hemophilia are genetic disorders, caused by abnormalities in the DNA of our own chromosomes.
item.itunes:summary:
Episode 007
Feeling sick? Although many illnesses are caused by microorganisms, other diseases such as cystic fibrosis and hemophilia are genetic disorders, caused by abnormalities in the DNA of our own chromosomes.

item.title: Carbon Nanotubes
item.itunes:author: Tiny Tech Radio Series
item.itunes:subtitle: A key ingredient in many products, from pencil leads to tennis racquets, is the chemical substance graphite. Graphite is a form of carbon in which the atoms bond to one another in sheets that are one atom thick.
item.itunes:summary:
Episode 008
A key ingredient in many products, from pencil leads to tennis racquets, is the chemical substance graphite. Graphite is a form of carbon in which the atoms bond to one another in sheets that are one atom thick.

item.title: Green Nanomaterials
item.itunes:author: Tiny Tech Radio Series
item.itunes:subtitle: The topic is green nanomaterials. Like other nanomaterials, they're made from nano-sized components, but they are especially friendly to the environment.
item.itunes:summary: Episode 009 The topic is green nanomaterials. Like other nanomaterials, they're made from nano-sized components, but they are especially friendly to the environment.

item.title: Quantum Dots
item.itunes:author: Tiny Tech Radio Series
item.itunes:subtitle: I don't know about you, but I get crabby if my shoes are too small for my feet.Well, electrons don't get crabby, but they do change properties if they are confined in small spaces.The small spaces that scientists are using to...
item.itunes:summary:
Episode 010
I don't know about you, but I get crabby if my shoes are too small for my feet.Well, electrons don't get crabby, but they do change properties if they are confined in small spaces.The small spaces that scientists are using to...

item.title: Scanning Probe Microscopy
item.itunes:author: Tiny Tech Radio Series
item.itunes:subtitle: What's the smallest thing you can see? Well, ordinary microscopes can image things that are about 100 times smaller than a human hair, which actually is still pretty big on the nano scale.For smaller objects, you...
item.itunes:summary:
Episode 011
What's the smallest thing you can see? Well, ordinary microscopes can image things that are about 100 times smaller than a human hair, which actually is still pretty big on the nano scale.For smaller objects, you...

item.title: Nanostructured Catalysts
item.itunes:author: Tiny Tech Radio Series
item.itunes:subtitle: If you are in the business of making plastics, or gasoline, or pharmaceuticals, you quickly discover that the chemical reactions to make them are really slow: you can mix the reactants and wait years -- and nothing...
item.itunes:summary:
Episode 012
If you are in the business of making plastics, or gasoline, or pharmaceuticals, you quickly discover that the chemical reactions to make them are really slow: you can mix the reactants and wait years -- and nothing...

item.title: The Feat of Sticky Little Feet
item.itunes:author: Tiny Tech Radio Series
item.itunes:subtitle: Consider the gecko, a fascinating lizard. Geckos have the amazing ability to walk upside down even on the undersides of smooth surfaces such as glass.This ability is due to the millions of tiny flexible hairs on the undersides of their feet.
item.itunes:summary:
Episode 013
Consider the gecko, a fascinating lizard. Geckos have the amazing ability to walk upside down even on the undersides of smooth surfaces such as glass.This ability is due to the millions of tiny flexible hairs on the undersides of their feet.
item.enclosure: length="1435006" type="audio/mpeg" /> https://lmwhite.chem.ufl.edu/wp-content/uploads/sites/374/0013-Gecko-Feet.mp3" Fri, 14 July 2017 03:39:05 GMT 00:01:30 nanotechnology, computers

item.title: Luminescent Nanomaterials
item.itunes:author: Tiny Tech Radio Series
item.itunes:subtitle: Feeling energetic?Well, sometimes molecules end up with excess energy too.This can happen in several ways, such as when molecules absorb light, are subjected to an electrical current, or undergo a chemical...
item.itunes:summary:
Episode 014
Feeling energetic?Well, sometimes molecules end up with excess energy too.This can happen in several ways, such as when molecules absorb light, are subjected to an electrical current, or undergo a chemical...

item.title: Nanocar Race
item.itunes:author: Tiny Tech Radio Series
item.itunes:subtitle: How would you like to drive on a racetrack of solid gold or silver? Well, six teams of scientists recently did just that. However, it would take 100 of their racetracks, laid end-to-end, to equal the thickness of a piece of paper, and...
item.itunes:summary:
Episode 015
How would you like to drive on a racetrack of solid gold or silver? Well, six teams of scientists recently did just that. However, it would take 100 of their racetracks, laid end-to-end, to equal the thickness of a piece of paper, and...

item.title: Superparamagnetic Nanoparticles
item.itunes:author: Tiny Tech Radio Series
item.itunes:subtitle: The dark mag-stripe on your credit card is made of iron-containing particles, each of which is a magnet with a north and south pole. The information on your card is stored by making magnetic patterns, in which the poles...
item.itunes:summary:
Episode 016
The dark mag-stripe on your credit card is made of iron-containing particles, each of which is a magnet with a north and south pole. The information on your card is stored by making magnetic patterns, in which the poles...

item.title: Titania Nanosheets
item.itunes:author: Tiny Tech Radio Series
item.itunes:subtitle: In the modern world, titania (also known by its chemical formula, TiO2) can be found in many products.This is because titania is great at absorbing UV light, making it ideal for use in protective coatings like paint or sunscreen...
item.itunes:summary:
Episode 017
In the modern world, titania (also known by its chemical formula, TiO2) can be found in many products.This is because titania is great at absorbing UV light, making it ideal for use in protective coatings like paint or sunscreen...

item.title: Nanotechnology and Diabetes
item.itunes:author: Tiny Tech Radio Series
item.itunes:subtitle: There are many reasons for getting a tattoo: personal expression, remembrance of a family member, or just for fun. But what if there could be a medical reason for getting a tattoo? People with diabetes must...
item.itunes:summary:
Episode 018
There are many reasons for getting a tattoo: personal expression, remembrance of a family member, or just for fun. But what if there could be a medical reason for getting a tattoo? People with diabetes must...

item.title: Graphene: Tale of the Tape
item.itunes:author: Tiny Tech Radio Series
item.itunes:subtitle: Remember when you were a kid, drawing all over your desk with your trusty number 2 pencil and then trying to rub off the black graphite marks with your hands? Well, if you had tried to clean your blackened fingers...
item.itunes:summary:
Episode 019
Remember when you were a kid, drawing all over your desk with your trusty number 2 pencil and then trying to rub off the black graphite marks with your hands? Well, if you had tried to clean your blackened fingers...

item.title: Organic Solar Cells
item.itunes:author: Tiny Tech Radio Series
item.itunes:subtitle: Ever wonder how a solar cell works? When sunlight strikes a solar cell, its energy is transferred to electrons, which jump out of their original locations and move through the solar cell, creating an electrical current...
item.itunes:summary:
Episode 020
Ever wonder how a solar cell works? When sunlight strikes a solar cell, its energy is transferred to electrons, which jump out of their original locations and move through the solar cell, creating an electrical current...

item.title: The Amazing Spider Silk
item.itunes:author: Tiny Tech Radio Series
item.itunes:subtitle: The plastic items we know so well are made of polymers, materials consisting of simple repeating units of particular chemicals. As it turns out, nature is a brilliant polymer chemist...
item.itunes:summary:
Episode 021
The plastic items we know so well are made of polymers, materials consisting of simple repeating units of particular chemicals. As it turns out, nature is a brilliant polymer chemist...

item.title: Nanowire Barcodes
item.itunes:author: Tiny Tech Radio Series
item.itunes:subtitle: When I'm at the grocery store, I always seem to be in the slowest checkout line. But it's faster than it used to be because products come with a barcode on the label...
item.itunes:summary:
Episode 022
When I'm at the grocery store, I always seem to be in the slowest checkout line. But it's faster than it used to be because products come with a barcode on the label...

item.title: Aerogels Lighter than a Feather
item.itunes:author: Tiny Tech Radio Series
item.itunes:subtitle: In the movie Back to the Future, George McFly was a little confused when he told his future wife I am your density....
item.itunes:summary:
Episode 023
In the movie Back to the Future, George McFly was a little confused when he told his future wife I am your density....

item.title: Nanodiamonds are Forever
item.itunes:author: Tiny Tech Radio Series
item.itunes:subtitle: Diamonds are a Girl's Best Friend sang Marilyn Monroe in Gentleman Prefer Blondes. However nanodiamonds may become everyone's best friend!...
item.itunes:summary:
Episode 024
Diamonds are a Girl's Best Friend sang Marilyn Monroe in Gentleman Prefer Blondes. However nanodiamonds may become everyone's best friend!...

item.title: Photothermal Therapy Can You Take the Heat?
item.itunes:author: Tiny Tech Radio Series
item.itunes:subtitle: If the cells in your body get hot, they get a little uncomfortable and don't work as well. Unlike us, they can't just drink some ice-cold lemonade to cool off. Your cells can only live near body temperature, and if things get too hot, they die. This is true for normal cells, and also for cancer cells...
item.itunes:summary:
Episode 025
If the cells in your body get hot, they get a little uncomfortable and don't work as well. Unlike us, they can't just drink some ice-cold lemonade to cool off. Your cells can only live near body temperature, and if things get too hot, they die. This is true for normal cells, and also for cancer cells.

item.title: The Incredible Shrinking Gel
item.itunes:author: Tiny Tech Radio Series
item.itunes:subtitle: Remember that annoying cotton shirt that shrank two sizes after you did your laundry? Well, scientists have recently developed a really clever way to use a similar effect to make tiny three dimensional devices...
item.itunes:summary:
Episode 026
Remember that annoying cotton shirt that shrank two sizes after you did your laundry? Well, scientists have recently developed a really clever way to use a similar effect to make tiny three dimensional devices.

item.title: The Nanotechnology of Batteries
item.itunes:author: Tiny Tech Radio Series
item.itunes:subtitle: Batteries are an amazing technology. Each battery is a little chemical reactor: when power is needed, the chemical reaction proceeds, and electricity is generated. When the chemical reaction ends, the battery is dead. Of all the kinds of batteries, lithium-ion batteries have the greatest energy per unit weight, which is why they are used in such things as laptop computers...
item.itunes:summary:
Episode 027
Batteries are an amazing technology. Each battery is a little chemical reactor: when power is needed, the chemical reaction proceeds, and electricity is generated. When the chemical reaction ends, the battery is dead. Of all the kinds of batteries, lithium-ion batteries have the greatest energy per unit weight, which is why they are used in such things as laptop computers.

item.title: Nanodentistry
item.itunes:author: Tiny Tech Radio Series
item.itunes:subtitle: Teeth make eating possible, sharks scary, and smiles pretty. But when you have a cavity, you need to see a dentist. For over 200 years, dentists have been filling cavities with amalgams, which are mixtures of silver, mercury, and other metals. Since the 1970s, however, dentists have increasingly been using a new material called a composite, which is made by adding powdered quartz to a plastic resin...
item.itunes:summary:
Episode 028
Teeth make eating possible, sharks scary, and smiles pretty. But when you have a cavity, you need to see a dentist. For over 200 years, dentists have been filling cavities with amalgams, which are mixtures of silver, mercury, and other metals. Since the 1970s, however, dentists have increasingly been using a new material called a composite, which is made by adding powdered quartz to a plastic resin.

item.title: The Blue of a Spider
item.itunes:author: Tiny Tech Radio Series
item.itunes:subtitle: Got the blues? Well, someone who's really blue - but likes it - is the cobalt blue tarantula. This large spider gets its color not from a pigment, but from tiny structures in its hair, which cause the reflected light to display an effect called interference. The same interference effect causes soap bubbles, opals, and butterfly wings to display many different colors depending on what angle you view them from. What is remarkable about the cobalt blue tarantula is that the color doesn't change in this way, but stays the same...
item.itunes:summary:
Episode 029
Got the blues? Well, someone who's really blue -- but likes it -- is the cobalt blue tarantula. This large spider gets its color not from a pigment, but from tiny structures in its hair, which cause the reflected light to display an effect called interference. The same interference effect causes soap bubbles, opals, and butterfly wings to display many different colors depending on what angle you view them from. What is remarkable about the cobalt blue tarantula is that the color doesn't change in this way, but stays the same.

item.title: Molecules and the Modern-Day Sherlock Holmes
item.itunes:author: Tiny Tech Radio Series
item.itunes:subtitle: The 1930s radio version of the great fictional detective, Sherlock Holmes, often exclaimed Elementary, my dear Watson! when he reached an obvious conclusion. What you might not know, is that Sherlock Holmes was an expert chemist, often carrying out chemical experiments as part of his detective work...
item.itunes:summary:
Episode 030
The 1930s radio version of the great fictional detective, Sherlock Holmes, often exclaimed Elementary, my dear Watson! when he reached an obvious conclusion. What you might not know, is that Sherlock Holmes was an expert chemist, often carrying out chemical experiments as part of his detective work.
item.enclosure: Download .mp3" length="1435006" type="audio/mpeg" /> https://lmwhite.chem.ufl.edu/wp-content/uploads/sites/374/0030-SERS-analysis.mp3">Download .mp3 Wed, 8 January 2020 03:39:05 GMT 00:01:30 nanotechnology, computers

item.title: 3D Nanoprinting with an Electron Beam Pen
item.itunes:author: Tiny Tech Radio Series
item.itunes:subtitle: These days, you can use a 3D printer to make almost anything from a missing part for a broken toy to a prosthetic leg! Zooming down to the nanoscale, scientists are also developing ways of using 3D printing to make very tiny objects...
item.itunes:summary:
Episode 031
These days, you can use a 3D printer to make almost anything from a missing part for a broken toy to a prosthetic leg! Zooming down to the nanoscale, scientists are also developing ways of using 3D printing to make very tiny objects.

item.title: Chemical Bonding on Video
item.itunes:author: Tiny Tech Radio Series
item.itunes:subtitle: Chemical bonds are small, so small in fact that we can't see them with the most powerful optical microscopes. That's because the wavelength of visible light is about 400 times larger than the usual bond length. That's where electrons come in. Electrons are wave-like particles that are tiny, just like bonds, and can be used instead of light. When the sample is thin enough, electrons travel through the material and are collected on the other side by a detector that produces an image...
item.itunes:summary:
Episode 032
Chemical bonds are small, so small in fact that we can't see them with the most powerful optical microscopes. That's because the wavelength of visible light is about 400 times larger than the usual bond length. That's where electrons come in. Electrons are wave-like particles that are tiny, just like bonds, and can be used instead of light. When the sample is thin enough, electrons travel through the material and are collected on the other side by a detector that produces an image.

item.title: Antibacterial Nanostructures
item.itunes:author: Tiny Tech Radio Series
item.itunes:subtitle: Looking for a new way to kill germs? Nanotechnology might hold the answer. Bacteria are becoming more resistant to current drugs, making it harder to treat diseases caused by them. There is constant research into making new molecules to fight these more resistant pathogens, however there is always the chance that the bacteria will eventually adjust to new drugs in turn. Nanotechnology offers a different approach and recent advances are starting to bring their use closer to reality...
item.itunes:summary:
Episode 033
Looking for a new way to kill germs? Nanotechnology might hold the answer. Bacteria are becoming more resistant to current drugs, making it harder to treat diseases caused by them. There is constant research into making new molecules to fight these more resistant pathogens, however there is always the chance that the bacteria will eventually adjust to new drugs in turn. Nanotechnology offers a different approach and recent advances are starting to bring their use closer to reality

item.title: Nanotube Recycling of Electronic Waste
item.itunes:author: Tiny Tech Radio Series
item.itunes:subtitle: Got your cell phone with you? I thought so. Like many other everyday electronic devices we cannot live without, your phone contains tiny amounts of scarce but critical rare earth elements, such as praseodynium and europium. Our seemingly insatiable appetite for the latest electronics means that the current global demand for these elements is far greater than theavailable supply...
item.itunes:summary:
Episode 034
Got your cell phone with you? I thought so. Like many other everyday electronic devices we cannot live without, your phone contains tiny amounts of scarce but critical rare earth elements, such as praseodynium and europium. Our seemingly insatiable appetite for the latest electronics means that the current global demand for these elements is far greater than theavailable supply

item.title: Nanoparticles in Ancient Art
item.itunes:author: Tiny Tech Radio Series
item.itunes:subtitle: Nanotechnology is considered an example of the latest in modern science. But you might be surprised to know that one particular kind of nanotechnology has been around for a very long time. The Lycurgus cup is a small glass goblet decorated with an image of the mythical king Lycurgus. It was made around 300 AD, possibly in Rome, and today is owned by the British Museum in London...
item.itunes:summary:
Episode 035
Nanotechnology is considered an example of the latest in modern science. But you might be surprised to know that one particular kind of nanotechnology has been around for a very long time. The Lycurgus cup is a small glass goblet decorated with an image of the mythical king Lycurgus. It was made around 300 AD, possibly in Rome, and today is owned by the British Museum in London.

item.title: An Artificial Leaf?
item.itunes:author: Tiny Tech Radio Series
item.itunes:subtitle: To satisfy our future need for energy, it will be increasingly important to use renewable energy sources, such as sunlight. Plants have been using sunlight as an energy source for millions of years. In a process called photosynthesis, plants use the sun's energy to make cellulose and other chemicals they need...
item.itunes:summary:
Episode 036
To satisfy our future need for energy, it will be increasingly important to use renewable energy sources, such as sunlight. Plants have been using sunlight as an energy source for millions of years. In a process called photosynthesis, plants use the sun's energy to make cellulose and other chemicals they need.

item.title: Wood Lights
item.itunes:author: Tiny Tech Radio Series
item.itunes:subtitle: You can light fires with wood, but how about wood that emits light without burning? Scientists from New Zealand recently took thin sheets of balsa wood and extracted them with a solvent to dissolve away some of the more soluble components. They then filled the holes in the resulting porous, spongy solid with nano-sized luminescent quantum dots...
item.itunes:summary:
Episode 037
You can light fires with wood, but how about wood that emits light without burning? Scientists from New Zealand recently took thin sheets of balsa wood and extracted them with a solvent to dissolve away some of the more soluble components. They then filled the holes in the resulting porous, spongy solid with nano-sized luminescent quantum dots.

item.title: ITO or Graphene
item.itunes:author: Tiny Tech Radio Series
item.itunes:subtitle: Cell phones and other mobile electronic devices are everywhere. Manufacturing them in large numbers, however, depends on the availability of rare elements that increasingly are in short supply. For example, the light emitting material in display screens must be situated between two layers of electrodes...
item.itunes:summary:
Episode 038
Cell phones and other mobile electronic devices are everywhere. Manufacturing them in large numbers, however, depends on the availability of rare elements that increasingly are in short supply. For example, the light emitting material in display screens must be situated between two layers of electrodes, and one of these electrodes must be transparent.

item.title: A Suit of Alloy
item.itunes:author: Tiny Tech Radio Series
item.itunes:subtitle: If you enjoy comics or superhero movies, you've probably heard of someone who's just a genius, billionaire, playboy, philanthropist when you take away his metal suit. But our superhero admitted that he liked the name iron man, even though the fictional suit was actually made of gold-titanium alloy...
item.itunes:summary:
Episode 039
If you enjoy comics or superhero movies, you've probably heard of someone who's just a genius, billionaire, playboy, philanthropist when you take away his metal suit. But our superhero admitted that he liked the name iron man, even though the fictional suit was actually made of gold-titanium alloy.

item.title: Seeing through Liquids with an Electron Microscope
item.itunes:author: Tiny Tech Radio Series
item.itunes:subtitle: When scientists take pictures or movies of atom-sized objects, they often use a T.E.M. a type of electron microscope that transmits an electron beam through the sample. At first, however, only solids could have their picture taken...
item.itunes:summary: Episode 040 When scientists take pictures or movies of atom-sized objects, they often use a T.E.M. a type of electron microscope that transmits an electron beam through the sample. At first, however, only solids could have their picture taken, because liquids tend to evaporate into the vacuum that the electron beam needs to travel through.

item.title: Environmental Cleanup with Metal-Organic Frameworks
item.itunes:author: Tiny Tech Radio Series
item.itunes:subtitle: Cleaning up environmental pollutants is a big problem that needs "out of the box" answers. Recently, scientists have been investigating a new kind of material called a metal-organic framework or MOF...
item.itunes:summary:
Episode 041
Cleaning up environmental pollutants is a big problem that needs "out of the box" answers. Recently, scientists have been investigating a new kind of material called a metal-organic framework or MOF for water purification.
item.enclosure: Download .mp3" length="1435006" type="audio/mpeg" /> https://lmwhite.chem.ufl.edu/wp-content/uploads/sites/374/0041-MOFs.mp3">Download .mp3 Fri, 15 July 2022 16:39:05 GMT 00:01:30 nanotechnology, computers

item.title: Nanofiber bandages: Let's not all stick together
item.itunes:author: Tiny Tech Radio Series
item.itunes:subtitle: Skinned knees are bad enough, but why is taking a bandage off sometimes as painful as the original accident? Well, when moms (and doctors) treat a large skin wound...
item.itunes:summary:
Episode 042
Skinned knees are bad enough, but why is taking a bandage off sometimes as painful as the original accident? Well, when moms (and doctors) treat a large skin wound...

item.title: Say Goodbye to Needles
item.itunes:author: Tiny Tech Radio Series
item.itunes:subtitle: Have you ever wished you could get needed medical injections without being poked with a needle? Well, instead of using needles, hand-held devices called jet injectors use high-pressure puffs of gases...
item.itunes:summary:
Episode 043
Have you ever wished you could get needed medical injections without being poked with a needle? Well, instead of using needles, hand-held devices called jet injectors use high-pressure puffs of gases or liquids to deliver medicines just under the skin...

item.title: Beer and Magnetic BioBots
item.itunes:author: Tiny Tech Radio Series
item.itunes:subtitle: Making beer by fermentation of barley and hops has been around for centuries. But could nanotechnology improve the process? Recently, to help with making beer, scientists have developed nanostructures called BioBots...
item.itunes:summary:
Episode 044
Making beer by fermentation of barley and hops has been around for centuries. But could nanotechnology improve the process? Recently, to help with making beer, scientists have developed nanostructures called BioBots...

item.title: Carbon Quantum Dots
item.itunes:author: Tiny Tech Radio Series
item.itunes:subtitle: In nanotechnology, the big ideas involve really small things, such as quantum dots. Quantum dots are very small chunks of matter that can be tuned to absorb or emit light of different colors simply by changing the size of the dot...
item.itunes:summary:
Episode 045
In nanotechnology, the big ideas involve really small things, such as quantum dots. Quantum dots are very small chunks of matter that can be tuned to absorb or emit light of different colors simply by changing the size of the dot...

item.title: Slick as a Whistle
item.itunes:author: Tiny Tech Radio Series
item.itunes:subtitle: Spray-on windshield coatings that repel rain are really slick: they consist of a thin film of silicone, the same rubbery material used as caulking around the home. Silicones are hydrophobic, which means that they repel water, but some materials repel water so strongly that they are called superhydrophobic....
item.itunes:summary:
Episode 046
Spray-on windshield coatings that repel rain are really slick: they consist of a thin film of silicone, the same rubbery material used as caulking around the home. Silicones are hydrophobic, which means that they repel water, but some materials repel water so strongly that they are called superhydrophobic....

item.title: Nano-fingerprints: Combating Counterfeits
item.itunes:author: Tiny Tech Radio Series
item.itunes:subtitle: How do you prove that you are you? One way is to take advantage of the fact that your fingerprints make a pattern that is uniquely yours. But how do we prove that the things around us from dollar bills to documents to pharmaceuticals are genuine and not counterfeits?...
item.itunes:summary:
Episode 047
How do you prove that you are you? One way is to take advantage of the fact that your fingerprints make a pattern that is uniquely yours. But how do we prove that the things around us from dollar bills to documents to pharmaceuticals are genuine and not counterfeits?...

item.title: The Cozy Desert Chameleon
item.itunes:author: Tiny Tech Radio Series
item.itunes:subtitle: What's warm when its cold and cool when it's hot? One surprising answer is the Namibian chameleon! This chameleon can control how much sunlight it absorbs by changing its skin color...
item.itunes:summary: Episode 048 What's warm when it's cold and cool when it's hot? One surprising answer is the Namibian chameleon! This chameleon can control how much sunlight it absorbs by changing its skin color...

item.title: Treating Acne by Getting Under Your Skin
item.itunes:author: Tiny Tech Radio Series
item.itunes:subtitle: Everyone – especially teenagers – hates spots caused by acne. The bacteria that cause acne can be killed by antibiotics, but a big problem is the eventual development of antibiotic resistance...
item.itunes:summary: Episode 049 Everyone – especially teenagers – hates spots caused by acne. The bacteria that cause acne can be killed by antibiotics, but a big problem is the eventual development of antibiotic resistance...

item.title: Flow Batteries
item.itunes:author: Tiny Tech Radio Series
item.itunes:subtitle: Over 20% of all electricity in the US is now generated by renewable wind and solar power. That’s impressive, but wind turbines and solar panels are fickle: sometimes they generate more electricity than is needed, and other times none at all...
item.itunes:summary: Episode 050 Over 20% of all electricity in the US is now generated by renewable wind and solar power. That’s impressive, but wind turbines and solar panels are fickle: sometimes they generate more electricity than is needed, and other times none at all...

item.title: Computers from DNA?
item.itunes:author: Tiny Tech Radio Series
item.itunes:subtitle: Scientists and engineers are always looking for ways to make computers more powerful. Some have speculated that this might be possible using DNA, the molecules in our bodies that store genetic information...
item.itunes:summary: Episode 051 Scientists and engineers are always looking for ways to make computers more powerful. Some have speculated that this might be possible using DNA, the molecules in our bodies that store genetic information....

item.title: Real-Life Spider-Man Web Shooter
item.itunes:author: Tiny Tech Radio Series
item.itunes:subtitle: In comic books and movies, Spider-Man uses wrist-worn devices called Web Shooters to capture enemies and swing between buildings at high speeds. The Web Shooters emit streams of liquid, which solidify and stick to the intended target. Awesome! But is this possible in real life?...
item.itunes:summary: Episode 052 In comic books and movies, Spider-Man uses wrist-worn devices called Web Shooters to capture enemies and swing between buildings at high speeds. The Web Shooters emit streams of liquid, which solidify and stick to the intended target. Awesome! But is this possible in real life?....

item.title: Making Tiny Patterns with Lasers and Nanoparticles
item.itunes:author: Tiny Tech Radio Series
item.itunes:subtitle: Scientists and engineers are always trying to develop better ways to make patterns on surfaces. Such patterns are the basis for how lots of things work...
item.itunes:summary: Episode 053 Scientists and engineers are always trying to develop better ways to make patterns on surfaces. Such patterns are the basis for how lots of things work....

item.title: Wearable Sensors for Monitoring Cancer
item.itunes:author: Tiny Tech Radio Series
item.itunes:subtitle: Doctors know that a treatment for cancer is probably working if the tumor shrinks in size. Today, tumor size is usually measured every couple of months or so, but it would be useful if the size could be monitored continuously...
item.itunes:summary: Episode 054 Doctors know that a treatment for cancer is probably working if the tumor shrinks in size. Today, tumor size is usually measured every couple of months or so, but it would be useful if the size could be monitored continuously...

item.title: DNA Nanoturbine
item.itunes:author: Tiny Tech Radio Series
item.itunes:subtitle: DNA is the blueprint of life – the genetic code that shapes every living organism. But beyond genetics, DNA can be used not just for making humans: it can also be used for making tiny machines, such as turbines...
item.itunes:summary: Episode 055 DNA is the blueprint of life – the genetic code that shapes every living organism. But beyond genetics, DNA can be used not just for making humans: it can also be used for making tiny machines, such as turbines...

item.title: Super Sharp Microscope Tips
item.itunes:author: Tiny Tech Radio Series
item.itunes:subtitle: Have you ever closed your eyes and tried to identify an object by running your fingers over it? This is what an atomic force microscope does but on a very small scale...
item.itunes:summary: Episode 056 Have you ever closed your eyes and tried to identify an object by running your fingers over it? This is what an atomic force microscope does but on a very small scale...

channel.item:
Nanomaterials in Construction Tiny Tech Radio Series What would it be like to have self-healing walls of your building? Or that the concrete you are standing on would last decades without cracking because it contains particles smaller than a virus?... Episode 057 What would it be like to have self-healing walls of your building? Or that the concrete you are standing on would last decades without cracking because it contains particles smaller than a virus?...
<guidhttps://lmwhite.chem.ufl.edu/wp-content/uploads/sites/374/0057-Nano-in-construction.mp3 Fri, 08 May 2026 16:39:05 GMT 00:01:30 nanotechnology, computers
